title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
short_title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get extra time to file their taxes when natural disasters happen. Right now, only disasters declared by the federal government can delay tax deadlines, but this bill would let state governors also request tax deadline delays for disasters they declare in their states. The bill covers disasters like hurricanes, earthquakes, floods, and fires that cause serious damage. It also gives people affected by disasters more time by extending the automatic deadline extension from 60 days to 120 days.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Modification of Rules for Postponing Certain Deadlines by Reason of Disaster
sections.text: This section changes the tax rules about delaying tax deadlines when disasters happen. First, it allows the Secretary of the Treasury to delay federal tax deadlines when a state governor or the mayor of Washington DC declares a state disaster. The governor or mayor must ask in writing for this help. A qualified state declared disaster means any natural disaster like hurricanes, tornadoes, storms, high water, wind-driven water, tidal waves, tsunamis, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, mudslides, snowstorms, or droughts. It also includes fires, floods, or explosions no matter what caused them. The governor or mayor must decide that the damage is serious enough to need this tax deadline relief. The word state includes Washington DC, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Second, this section extends the required deadline extensions from sixty days to one hundred twenty days. Third, these changes will apply to disaster declarations made after this law is passed.
